readings.title: First reading
readings.text: It was this salvation that the prophets were looking and searching so hard for; their prophecies were about the grace which was to come to you. The Spirit of Christ which was in them foretold the sufferings of Christ and the glories that would come after them, and they tried to find out at what time and in what circumstances all this was to be expected. It was revealed to them that the news they brought of all the things which have now been announced to you, by those who preached to you the Good News through the Holy Spirit sent from heaven, was for you and not for themselves. Even the angels long to catch a glimpse of these things.

exegesis.summary: St. Peter marvels that the prophets of old searched diligently for the grace now revealed in Christ — the sufferings and glories foretold by the Spirit. The apostle reminds the baptized that they are the privileged recipients of what angels themselves long to glimpse, and therefore he calls them to lives of holiness and sober vigilance, conformed to the Holy One who has called them.

patristicQuotes.quote: By the Spirit of truth the knowledge of future events has been granted to holy men. Prophets filled with this Spirit foretold and saw things to come as if they were present.

Many filled with the grace of the Holy Spirit have foreknown future events; the Lord did inform the Apostles of what was to come, the evils they would suffer for Christ's sake and the good things they would receive in recompense.

cccReferences.title: The Spirit speaks through the prophets toward Christ
Two prophetic lines converge in the Remnant
The Spirit promised rests upon the Messiah
Angels serve the divine plan of salvation

exegesis.thematicConnection: Peter shows that the salvation prophesied of old has now been made known — exactly what the Psalm sings: 'The Lord has made known his salvation.' This salvation, revealed first to the prophets, is the very thing the disciples in the Gospel have left everything to follow.

readings.title: Responsorial Psalm
readings.text:
The Lord has made known his salvation.
Sing a new song to the Lord
for he has worked wonders.
His right hand and his holy arm
have brought salvation.
The Lord has made known his salvation.
The Lord has made known his salvation;
has shown his justice to the nations.
He has remembered his truth and love
for the house of Israel.
The Lord has made known his salvation.
All the ends of the earth have seen
the salvation of our God.
Shout to the Lord, all the earth,
ring out your joy.
The Lord has made known his salvation.
readings.refrain: The Lord has made known his salvation.

exegesis.summary: Psalm 98 is a coronation hymn celebrating the saving 'right hand' of the Lord. What was once Israel's hidden hope has now been unveiled to the ends of the earth, and all creation is summoned to a new song. The refrain anchors the Church's joy: the salvation prophets sought has been disclosed in Christ.

patristicQuotes.quote: The Lord Jesus is salvation, but the knowledge of salvation, that is, of Christ, was given in John, who bore witness of Christ.
By 'Hosanna,' that is, Salvation in the highest, it is clearly shown that the coming of Christ is not the salvation of man only, but of the whole world, joining earthly things to things heavenly.

cccReferences.title: God wills the salvation of all
Salvation flows from God's initiative of love
The Word of God is the power of God for salvation

exegesis.thematicConnection: The Psalm proclaims publicly what the First Reading says was once hidden in prophecy: God's saving arm revealed. It also frames the Gospel's promise of a hundredfold reward — the salvation that meets those who leave all for Christ.

readings.title: Gospel
readings.text: At that time Peter began to tell Jesus, ‘What about us? We have left everything and followed you.’ Jesus said, ‘I tell you solemnly, there is no one who has left house, brothers, sisters, father, children or land for my sake and for the sake of the gospel who will not be repaid a hundred times over, houses, brothers, sisters, mothers, children and land – not without persecutions – now in this present time and, in the world to come, eternal life.

exegesis.summary: After the rich young man walks away sorrowful, Peter speaks for the Twelve: 'We have left everything.' Jesus answers with a solemn promise: every renunciation made for him and for the Gospel will be repaid a hundredfold even now — in a new family, the Church — together with persecutions, and in the age to come, eternal life. The discipleship of detachment is not loss but exchange.

patristicQuotes.quote: It is not sufficient to have left all; he adds that which makes up perfection: 'and have followed thee.' Our Lord does not mean that we should leave our fathers without helping them, but that we should prefer the glory of God to the things of this world.

Because the youth had gone away sorrowful at the advice of our Saviour concerning the casting away of his goods, the disciples, who had already fulfilled the foregoing precept, began to question Him concerning their reward.

cccReferences.title: Peter, foundation rock of the Church
The Cross belongs to discipleship
Through many persecutions we enter the Kingdom
Faith hopes through tribulation

exegesis.thematicConnection: The reward Peter is promised is precisely the 'salvation made known' that the Psalm celebrates and the prophets sought (1 Peter). The 'persecutions' belong to those 'sufferings of Christ' foretold by the Spirit — and the 'glories that would come after them' are the hundredfold and eternal life.

readings.title: First reading
readings.text: I want you to be happy, always happy in the Lord; I repeat, what I want is your happiness. Let your tolerance be evident to everyone: the Lord is very near.

exegesis.summary: St. Paul commands a paradoxical joy — 'rejoice always' — grounded not in circumstance but in the nearness of the Lord. He calls the Philippians to gentleness, to prayer with thanksgiving that yields the peace 'which surpasses understanding,' and to fix the mind on whatever is true, noble, and pure. Christian happiness is the fruit of a heart anchored in Christ and trained to dwell on what is good.

patristicQuotes.quote: If at any time light shall have crept into his heart, and loving God and despising bodily things he shall have gained the perfect standing of the just, without any difficulty shall he obtain joy in the Lord.
To know what is good and not to do it tends not to happiness but to condemnation. Wherefore He adds: 'If ye know these things, happy are ye if ye do them.'

cccReferences.title: The natural desire for happiness is of divine origin
Our heart is restless until it rests in God
True happiness is the vision of God

exegesis.thematicConnection: Paul's exhortation to joy and peace is the lived experience of Jesus' high-priestly prayer in the Gospel: that the love of the Father may be in us. The Psalm answers in kind — 'Taste and see that the Lord is good' — for the one who seeks refuge in him lacks no blessing.

readings.title: Responsorial Psalm
readings.text:
I will bless the Lord at all times.
or
Taste and see that the Lord is good.
I will bless the Lord at all times,
his praise always on my lips;
in the Lord my soul shall make its boast.
The humble shall hear and be glad.
I will bless the Lord at all times.
or
Taste and see that the Lord is good.
Glorify the Lord with me.
Together let us praise his name.
I sought the Lord and he answered me;
from all my terrors he set me free.
I will bless the Lord at all times.
or
Taste and see that the Lord is good.
Look towards him and be radiant;
let your faces not be abashed.
This poor man called, the Lord heard him
and rescued him from all his distress.
I will bless the Lord at all times.
or
Taste and see that the Lord is good.
The angel of the Lord is encamped
around those who revere him, to rescue them.
Taste and see that the Lord is good.
He is happy who seeks refuge in him.
I will bless the Lord at all times.
or
Taste and see that the Lord is good.
Revere the Lord, you his saints.
They lack nothing, those who revere him.
Strong lions suffer want and go hungry
but those who seek the Lord lack no blessing.
I will bless the Lord at all times.
or
Taste and see that the Lord is good.
readings.refrain: I will bless the Lord at all times.

exegesis.summary: Psalm 34 is the song of the rescued poor man. 'I sought the Lord and he answered me' is the testimony of every soul that has cast itself upon God's goodness. The Church places this psalm on our lips as the truest response to Paul's call to rejoice — and as the deepest taste of the unity for which Christ prays in the Gospel.

patristicQuotes.quote: He never refuses His blessings to them that pray, but rather stirs men up by His mercy not to faint in praying. Consider what a blessed privilege is granted thee, to talk with God in thy prayers.
As often as any of the brethren shall sound forth the word of sacred learning, we should with our whole heart, our mouths and hands, return praise to God.

cccReferences.title: Praise recognizes that God is God
Praise is entirely disinterested
Let the hearts of those who seek the Lord rejoice

exegesis.thematicConnection: 'Taste and see' is the sacramental echo of Christ's prayer that we be one in him: communion known by experience. The psalm makes Paul's 'rejoice always' concrete — blessing the Lord at all times, in every distress from which he sets us free.

readings.title: Gospel
readings.text:
Jesus raised his eyes to heaven and said:
‘Holy Father,
I pray not only for these,
but for those also
who through their words will believe in me.
May they all be one.
Father, may they be one in us,
as you are in me and I am in you,
so that the world may believe it was you who sent me.
I have given them the glory you gave to me,
that they may be one as we are one.
With me in them and you in me,
may they be so completely one
that the world will realise that it was you who sent me
and that I have loved them as much as you loved me.
Father, I want those you have given me
to be with me where I am,
so that they may always see the glory you have given me
because you loved me before the foundation of the world.
Father, Righteous One,
the world has not known you,
but I have known you,
and these have known that you have sent me.
I have made your name known to them
and will continue to make it known,
so that the love with which you loved me may be in them,
and so that I may be in them.’

exegesis.summary: On the night before he died, Jesus prays not only for the apostles but for all who will believe through their word — including us. His petition is staggering: that we be one as the Father and Son are one, drawn into the very communion of the Trinity, so that the world may believe. He desires that those the Father has given him be with him to behold his glory, and that the Father's love itself dwell in them.

patristicQuotes.quote: As the disciples were still sad, henceforth He addresses Himself to the Father to show the love which He had for them. He not only gives them what He has of His own, but entreats another for them, as a still further proof of His love.
He does not say, 'That they may be one with Us, as We are one,' but 'that they may be one' — that they may be one in their nature, as We are one in Ours.
After He has said that many should believe on Him through them, He speaks of the crowns in store for them: 'Father, I will that they also whom Thou hast given Me, be with Me where I am.'

cccReferences.title: God's love poured into our hearts
Christ longs to live in his members
Chosen in him before the foundation of the world
The filial prayer of Jesus

exegesis.thematicConnection: Jesus' prayer for unity is the answer to Paul's appeal for joy and peace: both flow from indwelling love. The 'glory the Father gave' Christ is the same salvation the Psalm sings of, now poured into his Body, the Church.

synthesis.title: The Salvation the Prophets Longed For Is Now Our Joy
synthesis.body: Today the Church gathers up the great arc of salvation history. St. Peter tells us that the prophets searched and the angels long to glimpse what we now possess: the sufferings and glories of Christ revealed in our midst. The Psalm responds with the new song of a world that has at last seen God's saving arm. Then in the Gospel from Mark, Peter himself stands at the threshold of that mystery, and Jesus assures him — and us — that nothing surrendered for the Gospel is lost; everything is multiplied a hundredfold, even amid persecutions, and crowned with eternal life. The second set of readings deepens this same promise from within. Paul commands a joy that does not depend on circumstances because 'the Lord is near,' and Psalm 34 invites us to taste and see the goodness of the One who rescues the poor man. In John 17, we overhear Christ's own priestly prayer: that we be drawn into the very oneness of Father and Son, that the love with which the Father loved him before the foundation of the world might dwell in us. To leave all for Christ is not deprivation — it is to be folded into the Trinity's own life, the salvation prophets sought and angels still adore.
